section.title: Potential theory and harmonic functions
section.p: Maxwell's equations were the broad capstone: fields, flux, circulation, sources, boundaries, and the identity d^2=0 all appeared together. This section is a quieter coda. It returns to one scalar field u(x,y,z), but now we read it with the whole book behind us.

A scalar field can be a temperature, an electric potential, a height, or a pressure. When its gradient field has no source inside a region, the field satisfies \Delta u=0. Those functions are called harmonic. They are the steady states of heat flow, the potentials of source-free fields, and the energy-minimizing shapes among all functions with the same boundary values.

subsection.title: The Laplacian as divergence of the gradient
subsection.p: Start with steady heat in a thin metal plate. Let u(x,y) be the temperature. Fourier's law says heat flows down the temperature gradient: \mathbf J=-k\nabla u, where k>0 is constant.

If there are no heat sources inside the plate and the temperature is no longer changing, then the local balance law from becomes 
abla\cdot\mathbf J=0. Substitute the flux law: 
abla\cdot(-k
abla u)=0. Since k is constant, -k
abla\cdot(
abla u)=0. So  oxed{
abla\cdot(
abla u)=0.}
This expression is the Laplacian: \boxed{\Delta u=\nabla\cdot(\nabla u).}

statement.p: For a twice differentiable scalar field u, the Laplacian of u is \boxed{\Delta u=\nabla\cdot(\nabla u).} In \mathbb R^2, \Delta u=u_{xx}+u_{yy}. In \mathbb R^3, \Delta u=u_{xx}+u_{yy}+u_{zz}.

Let u(x,y)=e^x\cos y. Then u_{xx}=e^x\cos y, while u_{yy}=-e^x\cos y. Therefore \Delta u=u_{xx}+u_{yy}=0. This field can describe a steady temperature pattern with no internal heat sources, provided the boundary temperatures are chosen to match it.

subsection.p: So the Laplacian is the coefficient of the exterior derivative of the gradient flux form. It measures the source strength of the gradient field.

subsection.title: Harmonic functions
subsection.p: A function with zero Laplacian gets its own name.

statement.p: Let u(x,y)=x^2-y^2. Then u_{xx}=2,\qquad u_{yy}=-2. So \Delta u=2-2=0. The graph is a saddle. Harmonic does not mean flat. It means that the upward bending in one direction balances the downward bending in another.

theorem.title: Harmonic functions have zero gradient flux through small boundaries

proof.p: In space, Stokes' theorem gives \int_{\partial E}\Phi_{\nabla u}=\int_E d\Phi_{\nabla u}=\int_E \Delta u\,dx\wedge dy\wedge dz. If \Delta u=0, every such boundary flux is 0.
Conversely, if the boundary flux is 0 for every small E, then \int_E \Delta u\,dV=0 for every small E. Since \Delta u is continuous, the same argument used in forces \Delta u=0 pointwise.
The plane proof is the same, with dA replacing dV.

subsection.p: The hypotheses matter. The function must be defined and smooth on the whole region where we apply the theorem.

example.title: A hidden source at a missing point

statement.p: On the punctured plane \mathbb R^2\setminus\{(0,0)\}, define u(x,y)=\log r,\qquad r=\sqrt{x^2+y^2}. Away from the origin, u is harmonic: \Delta u=0. But its gradient is \nabla u=\left(\frac{x}{r^2},\frac{y}{r^2}\right).

On the circle r=a, the outward unit normal is \widehat{\mathbf n}=\left(\frac{x}{a},\frac{y}{a}\right), and \nabla u\cdot\widehat{\mathbf n}=\frac{1}{a}. Since ds=a\,d\theta,
\int_{r=a}\nabla u\cdot\widehat{\mathbf n}\,ds \amp= \int_0^{2\pi}\frac1a(a\,d\theta) \amp= 2\pi.

This does not contradict the theorem. The disk r\le a contains the origin, where \log r is not defined. The missing point behaves like a hidden source. A theorem about smooth functions on a whole region cannot be applied across a hole in the domain.

subsection.p: This example is the same kind of warning we saw for closed but non-exact forms. A puncture in the region can carry information that no local derivative sees away from the puncture.

subsection.title: Mean value ideas
subsection.p: Harmonic functions have a surprising averaging property: the value at the center of a circle is the average value on the circle.

figure.caption: A harmonic function is balanced: its center value equals its circular average.

subsection.p: The example is not a coincidence.

statement.p: Let u be harmonic on a disk containing the closed disk (x-a)^2+(y-b)^2\le r^2. Then \boxed{u(a,b)=\frac1{2\pi}\int_0^{2\pi}u(a+r\cos\theta,b+r\sin\theta)\,d\theta.} That is, the value at the center equals the average value on every circle around it, as long as the whole closed disk lies inside the domain.

subsection.p: Again the hypotheses are real. The function must be harmonic on the whole disk.

statement.p: Let u(x,y)=x^2+y^2. On the circle x=r\cos\theta,\qquad y=r\sin\theta, we have u=r^2. So the average value on that circle is r^2. But u(0,0)=0. The mean value property fails. The reason is visible in the Laplacian: \Delta u=2+2=4. This function has positive source strength everywhere.

subsection.p: The mean value property has a powerful consequence.

statement.p: Let u be harmonic and continuous on a closed, bounded region, and harmonic in its interior. If u reaches a maximum at an interior point, then u is constant on the connected component containing that point.
The same statement holds for an interior minimum.

proof.title: Proof idea
proof.p: If u(a,b) is a maximum, every value on a small circle around (a,b) is at most u(a,b). But the average around that circle must equal u(a,b). The only way an average of numbers all no larger than u(a,b) can equal u(a,b) is if all those numbers equal u(a,b).
So u is constant on every small circle around the point. Repeating this argument pushes the constancy through the connected region.

subsection.p: A steady temperature with no internal heat sources cannot have a lonely hot spot inside. Its hottest and coldest values must come from the boundary, unless the whole plate is already at one temperature.

subsection.title: Gradient fields and energy minimization
subsection.p: Harmonic functions also arise from an energy principle.

Imagine a flexible membrane stretched over a wire frame. The boundary wire fixes the height of the membrane along the edge. Among all surfaces with that boundary height, the physical membrane chooses the one with least stretching energy. In the small-slope model, that energy is \boxed{\mathcal E[u]=\frac12\iint_R |\nabla u|^2\,dA.} This is called the Dirichlet energy.
The energy is small when u changes gently, and large when u changes sharply.
A simple example shows the minimization idea.

statement.p: Let R=[0,1]\times[0,1], and consider u(x,y)=x. Now perturb u by a smooth function \eta that vanishes on the boundary: u_\varepsilon=u+\varepsilon\eta=x+\varepsilon\eta. The boundary values do not change, because \eta=0 on \partial R.

Compute the energy:
\mathcal E[u_\varepsilon] \amp= \frac12\iint_R\left|(1,0)+\varepsilon\nabla\eta\right|^2\,dA \amp= \frac12\iint_R\left(1+2\varepsilon\eta_x+\varepsilon^2|\nabla\eta|^2\right)\,dA.
The middle term is \iint_R \eta_x\,dA. Integrate in x:
\iint_R \eta_x\,dA \amp= \int_0^1\int_0^1 \eta_x(x,y)\,dx\,dy \amp= \int_0^1\bigl[\eta(1,y)-\eta(0,y)\bigr]\,dy \amp= 0,
because \eta is zero on the boundary.

Therefore \boxed{\mathcal E[u_\varepsilon]=\mathcal E[u]+\frac{\varepsilon^2}{2}\iint_R|\nabla\eta|^2\,dA.} The added term is always nonnegative. So the ramp u=x has the smallest energy among these boundary-preserving perturbations.

subsection.p: The example works because \Delta x=0. The same principle holds for every harmonic function.

statement.p: Let u be harmonic on a smooth bounded region R, and let w be another function with the same boundary values as u. Write w=u+\eta, where \eta=0\quad\text{on }\partial R. Then \boxed{\mathcal E[w]\ge \mathcal E[u].} More precisely, \boxed{\mathcal E[u+\eta]=\mathcal E[u]+\frac12\iint_R|\nabla\eta|^2\,dA.}

proof.p:
Expand:
\mathcal E[u+\eta] \amp=  rac12\iint_R|
abla u+
abla\eta|^2\,dA \amp=  rac12\iint_R|
abla u|^2\,dA+\iint_R
abla u\cdot
abla\eta\,dA+ rac12\iint_R|
abla\eta|^2\,dA.
So \mathcal E[u+\eta]=\mathcal E[u]+\iint_R
abla u\cdot
abla\eta\,dA+ rac12\iint_R|
abla\eta|^2\,dA.

Green's identity gives \iint_R
abla u\cdot
abla\eta\,dA=\int_{\partial R}\eta\, rac{\partial u}{\partial n}\,ds-\iint_R\eta\,\Delta u\,dA. The boundary term is zero because \eta=0\quad	ext{on }\partial R. The interior term is zero because \Delta u=0. Thus \iint_R
abla u\cdot
abla\eta\,dA=0. Therefore \mathcal E[u+\eta]=\mathcal E[u]+ rac12\iint_R|
abla\eta|^2\,dA\ge \mathcal E[u].

subsection.p: The boundary condition is part of the theorem, not a technical afterthought.

example.title: Why the same boundary values matter

statement.p: On the unit square, the function u(x,y)=x has positive energy: \mathcal E[u]=\frac12\iint_R 1\,dA=\frac12. The function w(x,y)=0 has energy \mathcal E[w]=0. So w has smaller energy.
But w does not have the same boundary values as u. Along the right edge, u(1,y)=1, while w(1,y)=0. The minimization theorem compares only functions attached to the same boundary data.

subsection.p: A harmonic function can now be seen in several equivalent ways:

The same equation has become a statement about sources, averages, and energy. That is a good place for the chapter to end. What remains is not another new theorem, but a set of projects that lets the whole chapter work as a modeling language.
